descriptionSummary: Planning Bill. Planning Bill; Natural Environment Bill. Environment Southland

chunks.text: Welcome, Mike. We can see you there. Uh, we have Environment Southland with us as well. We can see the team there, from the deep south. Welcome to the Environment Select Committee this morning. Uh, look, you have got, 15 minutes with us. So look, we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your submission and the main points, and then we'll open up for questions.

And just for those following along today online, we are in two subcommittees today. So we have Subcommittee A and we have Subcommittee B. So, if there was a particular, submission you wanted to hear, just have a look on those different subcommittees. But look, I'll hand it over to you, to Environment Southland.

Uh, you can hear us all right? Yes, we can hear you. Thank you. Uh, that's very good. Uh, look, thank you chair and members of the committee for the opportunity to present, Southland's perspective on these important reforms. My name is Jeremy McIhal, chair of Environment Southland. With me are Phil Morrison, our deputy chair, and Hayley Fitchett, our general manager of strategy and reg- regulation.

We're supported by senior staff, who can assist with technical matters if required. Environment Southland is responsible for implementing national direction on, on the ground, across freshwater, land use, natural hazards, and flood protection, working in enduring partnership with mana whenua and our communities. With that context, I'll hand to our deputy chair, Phil Morrison, to outline the key issues we wish to highlight today.

Uh, thank you. So we support the intent of these reforms. The current system is too slow, too complex, and not delivering the outcomes New Zealand needs. But our central message is this: if these bills are not adjusted in a small number of critical areas, they risk replacing one set of problems with another set, more complex, less coherent, and harder to make workable in practice. You have our full written submission.

Today, we will focus on four issues we believe are decisive to whether this s- system succeeds or fails. Issue one, system fragmentation. The split between the planning bill and the natural environment bill creates a structural risk. On paper, the system is designed as a coherent funnel with aligned goals, national direction, and integrated plans. However, there is no single clear purpose across both bills to guide how these systems should operate together.

In practice, councils will be required to operate two legislative systems at once, each with potentially different purposes, tools, and decision pathways. If integration is not explicitly required and enforced, fragmentation will occur at three levels: at strategic planning level, at plan-making level, and most critically, at the consenting interface. And when that happens, the burden does not sit with central government. It sits with councils and communities trying to reconcile conflicting signals.

That will introduce delay, cost, and litigation, exactly what this reform is trying to remove. So our position is simple. If two acts proceed, integration must be a binding system principle, not an assumption. Issue two, centralization and regional workability.

We support strong national direction. Clear environmental limits and consistent standards are necessary. We also strongly support regional spatial planning. But the f- the bills go further than direction. They shift control. National instruments will determine most key settings, leaving limited space for regional judgment. The risk is not theoretical. We have seen in the past that highly prescribed national frameworks can be difficult to implement, expensive to administer, and poorly adapted to regional conditions.

In Southland, this matters. We are a resource-dependent region, where economy, environment, and infrastructure are tightly coupled with specific challenges such as wetland loss and cumulative land use effects. We have demonstrated that regional collaboration works, for example, through our intensive winter grazing work with stakeholders. This led to changes that made regulation more workable while still achieving environmental outcomes. So if regional flexibility is too constrained, councils will be held accountable for outcomes

that they no longer have the tools to influence. That represents a design failure, not an implementation failure. Issue three, environmental limits and pathways. We strongly support limits. They are essential. But limits alone do not improve environmental outcomes. Limits require pathways, data, staging, tools, and time to transition. Many critical elements, such as methodologies and management units, are yet to be defined, and in areas like freshwater, limits are not yet supported by complete or consistent

data. Without flexibility in how limits are applied and phased, we risk creating a system that is technically sound but lacks workability in practice.This creates a risk that limits are either set too conservatively, delaying outcomes, or too ambitiously, which makes them unworkable.

And neither of those outcomes builds confidence. We also note that prioritising caps ahead of broader tools such as catchment act- action plans risks undermining the very approaches that have proven effective in regions like ours. So we need a system that enables progress towards limits, not just compliance with limits. Issue four, regulatory relief. While we recognise the intent of regulatory relief, the proposal raises serious equity and practical concerns. It introduces a mechanism that effectively compensates for

environmental protections on a property by property basis, and this risks three outcomes. First, it undermines spatial planning by potentially fragmenting decisions. Second, the risk of creating inequity between landowners. And third, placing significant and uncertain financial liability on councils and rate payers.

For regions like Southland with a small rating base, that exposure is significant. In effect, the system begins to treat environmental protection as a cost to be offset rather than a foundation to be maintained. And that is a fundamental shift in the logic of the system, and it risks weakening public trust in that system. So we recommend that these provisions be reconsidered and that alternative mechanisms, potentially outside the core system, be explored to incentivise positive environmental actions such as biodiversity and wetland restoration.

To round out the points made by Councillor Morrison, a one size fits all approach risks increasing rather than decreasing regulatory workload. We would caution the committee about accepting the idea that the bills, as drafted, will significantly reduce regulatory workload for both the regulated and the regulator. Part of the bills, including the registration of permitted activities and the accompanying workload, will shift effort and expense rather than remove it.

In summary, this reform is ambitious and necessary, but its success will depend less on its intent and more on whether the system is coherent, integrated and workable in practice. We've highlighted four issues today: system in- integration, appropriate centralization, workable environmental limits, and regulatory relief. These are not peripheral issues, they are system defining choices. If addressed well, this reform can succeed. If not, we risk embedding a new generation of complexity into the system, one that is harder to implement

and harder for communities to trust. Thank you, and we welcome your questions. Look, thank you very much for that submission. Uh, very clear, and a, a really well written submission, so we appreciate your time on that. Uh, look, we've got a couple of questions. Uh, we'll move to Priyanka first.

Thank you. Um, and thanks for your submission today. Um, I had a quick question around environmental limits and the point that you've made in your submit- written submission, that you support regional councils a- and, you know, feel that they're best placed to set, environmental limits and implement them. We've had other submitters make the point around, nationwide consistency and the fact that this may not lead to nationwide consistency, and some asking for some sort of a national framework within which regional councils have some

flexibility to make changes, that are more suited to their area. What would your thoughts be on something like that? Thank you for the question. I'll direct this question to Karen Wilson, who's our General Manager of Science. Um, so I would, concur with the idea that, I guess, nationally consistent frameworks are not going to lead to nationally consistent outcomes.

Um, the environments that they get applied to, can be very different, across the country. So in order to sort of achieve a similar level of outcome, there needs to be taken into account the localized, the localized system. So yeah, I would concur with the other submissions, on that one.

So you would agree that some sort of a nationwide framework within which you can make some changes would be better than just regional councils setting limits? Agreed. Okay. Thank you. And David McLeod. Yes. And thank you, also for the submission that you've provided.

Um, you mentioned about regulatory relief. It's a, it's a very common, submission that's been brought before us itself. Um, and of course it's based on the premise of, any new, imposition placed on a, on a landowner, is not left out of pocket. They don't go backwards negatively, a, a major imposition. Um, what, what in your mind are some of the solutions?

You didn't quite get to the solutions. You, you spoke about that you don't believe it should be in, within this. Could you t- talk, talk about that a bit more? Uh, thank you for your question. Um, we spoke in our submission, about potentially looking at a positively framed system rather... So, in terms of incentivization- Uh, for improvements to particular aspects such as biodiversity, the enhancement of wetlands.

Uh, wetland loss, as we've identified in our submission as a, as a critical concern and matter within Southland. We are leaning in very heavily into non-regulatory methods. But the addition of positively framed incentivization tools within a regulatory framework we feel could be, a substantial improvement to the system to further double down on the good work that our communities, our farming communities, our runanga are already making in the restoration enhancement, of, of wetlands, for example. Similarly with other forms

of biodiversity in Southland. Thank you. Thank you. And one last question from Steve Abel. Thank you, Madam Chair. I just wanted you to expand on your comments regarding regulatory relief. You say that it's, not workable, that it seems to set councils up to fail, and risks undermining the natural environment.

I wonder if you can expand on some of those comments, please. Certainly. Some of my colleagues Oh, we seem to have lost your audio there. Um. Uh, we still can't hear you. No. Oh, okay. Um. Um, we can't hear you. No? Okay. Um. Oh, there we go. If you can move a bit closer, maybe we might be able to hear you to the speaker.

Okay. Just one moment. Move a bit closer. There we go. We can hear you, yeah. Yes. Yeah, okay. Otherwise I was hoping that Mike would jump in online. Um, so the questions are on the workability of regulatory relief. The challenge in Southland is that we have such a small ratings base that covers a substantial, area, and we would have to make extraordinarily difficult trade-off choices, because of the cost, of potentially for regulatory relief to us, either through direct financial cost or the opportunity cost of, for example, if we would use

ratings relief. So, you know, co- re- revenue forgone. Um, and so we would be concerned that we wouldn't then be able to fulfill our statutory obligations with respect of things like the protection of biodiversity, for example. Um, and it would be, in a governance sense, extraordinarily difficult.

Is there anything that my colleagues would like to add on that? I think central from all of... The only, only thought I would add is that if, that once you introduce regulatory relief, there's an asymmetry in the system in that there's no provision for environmental relief, and at some point in the future there is potential for, government to potentially consider rebalancing that asymmetry by introducing the concept of environmental relief as well. And I think longer term, that's something that we need to take account of and consider, the potential for that kind

of subsequent intervention. Look, thank you. Uh, we have met time, but we have many questions, but we do appreciate, a very thorough and insightful submission, you know, covering the, the many areas there for consideration. So thank you to you and your team today.

Uh, look, thanks, thanks for the opportunity to, to speak to you. And, and just in closing, I would just like to say, look, highly important this work, but it needs to take time to get it right. Make sure we get it right, and, if it takes a bit longer, the timelines don't matter here. It's a once in generation change, so that's all I'd like to say.

So thanks again for the opportunity. Look, no, we appreciate that, and we are very aware of the significance of, of this reform, so thank you. Thank you. So we now move to our next submitter, New Zealand Notable Trees Trust, who I can see there. Uh, Brad, welcome to the select committee this morning.

Look, you have 15 minutes with us, so we will pass it over to you to talk us through your submission, and then we'll open up for questions. Oh, thank you, Madam Chair and members of the committee for the, opportunity to, appear today. My name is Brad Kadvalider, and I'm speaking on behalf of the New Zealand Notable Trees Trust. The trust works nationally to identify and record and support the protection of Aotearoa, New Zealand's historic and notable trees, working with landowners, territorial authorities, and community. Our submission focuses

specifically on how the proposed resource management reforms may affect the identification and protection of historic and notable trees. Trees in general provide a broad range of well-documented benefits. They improve air quality, moderate urban heat, support biodiversity, and contribute to the physical and wellbeing of our communities. However, historic and notable trees occupy a very special category within the wider urban forest. These are often mature or veteran specimens with exception...

exceptional age, size, rarity, cultural association, or landscape prominence. Some are early plantings linked to the history of settlement. Others are remarkable indigenous specimens representing ecological commu- continuity. Unlike buildings and infrastructure, a mature tree cannot simply be replaced.

When a historic or notable tree is lost- The accumulated ecological landscape and heritage values developed over decades or centuries are permanently lost. These trees therefore function as a long-term environmental and cultural infrastructure within our communities.

It is also worth noting that New Zealand contains many tree specimens that are exceptional by international standards. Our cool temperate climate has allowed a, a number of early exotic plantings to reach dimensions rarely seen within their natural ranges.

At the same time, mature indigenous trees attract significant international attention because New Zealand's native flora is globally distinctive. Together, these trees form what is effectively a living environmental and cultural collection across the country. Their protection is therefore not simply a local amenity issue, it's part of the long-term stewardship of nationally si- significant natural assets.

Protection of notable trees has been part of New Zealand's planning system for decades. Most scheduled trees have been identified and protected with landowner knowledge, and in many cases were nominated by landowners themselves, often as a legacy for future generations.

Properties containing protected trees are commonly purchased in full knowledge of those protections. This means protection is not generally an unexpected regulatory imposition, it is an established part of the planning framework attached to those properties.

Ultimately, the trust's concerns is not whether reform occurs, but how it is implemented. There are three areas of concern the trust wish to highlight. Firstly, the regulatory relief provisions. If notable tree scheduling were to trigger regulatory relief mechanisms, councils may face fiscal and legal exposure. Over time, that risk could discourage councils from retaining or introducing notable tree protections.

Secondly, institutional responsibility. The current Resource Management Act clearly empowers territorial authorities to identify and protect historic and notable trees through district plans. The proposed legislation introduces uncertainty about whether this function remains clearly embedded in the planning system.

And our third concern, that there is a potential mismatch with heritage frameworks. Many notable trees are protected not only for historic heritage reasons, but also for botanical, ecological, landscape and amenity values. If protection were to rely primarily on heritage legislation, there is a risk that many currently protected trees would fall outside that framework.

There is also a practical governance issue. New Zealand currently does not have a consolidated national inventory of legally protected historic and notable trees. Protection currently occurs through district plan schedules maintained independently by territorial authorities. Without a verified national baseline identifying how many trees are protected, where they are protected, and the values for which they are recognized, it is difficult to assess the scale of any function transfer or reform.

Structural reform of this nature should proceed on the basis of a clearly established evidential foundation. The trust therefore recommends three key actions. Firstly, the legislation should explicitly retain statutory provisions, enabling territorial authorities to identify and schedule notable trees, and ensure that regulatory relief mechanisms do not discourage councils from retaining or introducing those protections.

Secondly, existing district plan schedules should transition into the new framework without loss of protection. And lastly, if responsibility for notable tree protection is to be altered or centralized, this should occur only after a nationally coordinated audit establishes a clear baseline of the protected estate.

In closing, historic and notable trees are long-lived public assets. Their protection is proportionate, widely understood, and embedded in New Zealand's planning practice. The trust's central point is simply that reform should strengthen, not inadvertently weaken, those long-standing protections.

With appropriate legislative clarification, these reforms present an opportunity to strengthen consistency, transparency, and long-term stewardship of the historic and notable trees in our communitiesThank you very much for the opportunity to, present today. I'd be happy to answer any questions from the committee. Thank you.

Well, thank you, Brad. And, and thank you for your submission, because obviously it's a very, interesting area- Thank you... that we haven't traversed actually, fully yet. So look, Steve has got a question, so we'll go first to Steve Abel. Yeah. Thank you so much. A, a really excellent submission, and you made it very clear. I, I wonder would it be correct to reflect that, the great value that those notable trees offer the community, I think particularly in urban environments, is an excellent example of a commons, if you like. Um, a thing that has

broad community value for its provision of beauty, shade, ecology, history, and therefore something that, that benefits us all and can't be captured by the simple idea of, the private property rights of the person on whose property the tree stands when that tree has outlived them and will outlive them.

Um, so this is a sort of an excellent example of why we need to have a protect- a special status for these nationally significant trees. Yes, absolutely. The, the, the, the values of, of these trees extend well beyond, the, the property boundaries. Uh, the community absolutely shares, in the stories of those trees and, they, they're, often... You know, when these trees come under threat, communities rise up, so it shows really the, the significance the co- community hold in the, these individual trees.

Can I do a quick sup on it, Madam Chair? We, we... Yeah, a quick sup. It's very quick. Uh, you've talked about a consolidated national inve- inventory. Yes. Do you think that's a thing we should do anyway, that that would be hugely beneficial? Because I realize that these schedules are held in disparate spaces. Yes, yes. We, we've tried to, you know, get councils to bring, you know, bring them together but, you know, they all basically work in- independently. Uh, trying to, search across the various district plans is, is quite a, an

onerous task. Some of the listings include avenues, so they might include, 90 trees in a, a single line listing- True... whereas another line listing would be one, a tree. Yes. And the other, the other aspect is that, some of these trees have, are listed for historic purposes and others for their general amenity.

So it's quite a... Yeah. Thank you. Well, now over to Kevin Maxwell. I think, I think that would be it. Thank you, Chair. Uh, Brad, I... In your comment you're saying, you know, it goes, the value of these trees goes well beyond, the private property, and as a community shares in the value. I'm aware of, trees being cut down before they hit the height in a place called Papamoa of, what would become automatically listed as a, a notable tree. Now, would you think that if a property owner who, had, say, a Norfolk Pine could see themselves being,

compensated for what you've called the community sharing in the value through regulatory relief might incentivize people to see, these trees, as value to them as well, be recognized by the community, not just the cost being held by the property owner?

Well, firstly, I'm not really aware that, that, there is a local authority that automatically protects the tree Uh, this is in, in the Tauranga City Council, this is what used to happen. It doesn't happen anymore, I grant you. Right. Yes. But it used to happen. Yeah. Yeah. That's right. It's- That, that gen- general prote- tree protection is, is no longer, possible for local authorities, but, um- But this is my point. If it was to be, uh...

If there was to be a compensation, people might see the value in, in allowing these trees to become, recognized on a list that the community shares. Uh, yes, I guess that would be correct. Uh, how other local authorities compensate, landowners as they share in the maintenance and inspection of those trees.

So there are, you know, many ways councils can incentivize. Look, thank you very much, Brad, for your submission today. Uh, we have met time. Uh, we appreciate your time, and that perspective, obviously on protecting our trees. Uh, very important submission, so thank you very much for that. We appreciate it.
Thank you very much for the time. Bye. So now we move to our next submitter, the Hastings District Council.

And I can see a couple of familiar faces there- ... from Hastings. Welcome. Welcome to the select committee this morning. Uh, look, you've got 15 minutes with us, so, we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your submission, and then we'll open up for questions. So over to you.

Uh, morning, and thank you for allowing us to add to what I understand is in excess of four hundred hours of submissions. Um, so we will try and keep this brief, and we, we look forward to receiving your questions at the end. Um, so I'm Wendy Shullam of the Hastings District Council, and to my right is Caleb Sutton, who is our environmental consents manager here at HDC.

Um, thank you first of all for the opportunity to speak today, and as you likely have gathered, I am appearing on behalf of the Hastings District Council. Um, our council is, very supportive in principle, and especially with the efficient and cost-effective planning and resource management system and the government's resource management reform, package objectives. They align really well with what we believe as well, particularly with relation to the regional spatial plans. Um, so while we are broadly supportive, our

submission today is going to focus on a few key concerns with the proposed bills. Um, and these, these topics will cover things like climate change, regional spatial planning, transitional arrangements, and the practicality of the new system for councils and communities. So what I'd like to do today is just briefly connect those concerns to what that actually means for us on the ground in terms of communities like Hastings. Uh, at present, we understand that approximately sixty-one percent of New Zealand is in some form of

recovery, and while we are three years on from Cyclone Gabrielle here in Heretaunga, the impact is still significant, and it still scars our lands and people. Um, and it's for that reason that I really want to begin today with a mention of the absence of any explicit reference to climate change within the goals of the bills. Um, this is a significant concern for Hastings, as many of our recent decisions have relied heavily on climate-related considerations.

In our view, climate change is not a peripheral issue for planning, it's central. Uh, climate change underpi- underpins for us coastal hazards across the Hawke's Bay coastline, and it is increasingly determining where and how we develop safely. In practical terms, it's about whether we are enabling homes, infrastructure, and investment in places that will still be safe and viable in thirty to fifty years' time. Without climate change explicitly recognized as a goal, we do not believe that the regional spatial plans can provide the strategic direction the

legislation intends. And without national direction, we risk fragmented and inconsistent responses, repeating the piecemeal outcomes under previous legislation, and that's something we would like to help avoid. Uh, that means that we could end up in situations where different regions make different calls about risk, and in some cases, that could mean continuing allow- allowing development in areas that will ultimately put people and property in harm's way. So while we ins- support the intent of the bills, we are also concerned about the lack of an overarching

statement clearly tying the two together. For Hastings, this is particularly important when balancing our highly productive land with growth and property rights. They are two tensions that we navigate carefully here in Heretaunga. Our urban areas are surrounded by Class One and Two soils, and protecting them is central to our economy. These soils are not just land, they are the foundation of our horticulture, sector, our jobs, and our region's prosperity. Without clear integrated purpose, these matters may be treated

inconsistently. And on the ground, that creates uncertainty for landowners, investors, and our councils, and it increases the likelihood of conflict, delays, and costly legislation. Litigation. Uh, oh, sorry, litigation. Apologies. Um, with regards to the regional spatial plans, we are concerned about the key national direction, and how it may not be available in time to inform the first generation of regional spatial plans. Uh, to ensure those plans achieve the intended outcomes, we suggest, extending the statutory

time frames until the national direction is in place. Uh, preparing these plans are co- are costly and complex exercises, significantly more than the future development strategies, a process we've just been through. And in our experience, we suggest that it will require at least two years to complete. If we are required to move too quickly without clear direction, we risk doing the work twice, once to meet deadlines, and again to fix it later.

And in our view, that's not good use of ratepayer funding. Sufficient time is also needed for meaningful community, iwi, and hapū participation. If we get this stage wrong, we may lock in poor planning decisions for decades. We're also concerned about the proposed regulatory, relief regime, which I know I've heard other submitters, making points about as well, but I will reiterate from our perspective.

Uh, we believe that mandated or nationally directed provisions should not trigger relief. Provisions already settled under the RMA should be excluded, and there is a real risk of increased, litigation, under the current framework. In simple terms, this could inadvertently create a system where councils are required to compensate for rules that are legally required-- we are legally required to implement. That is a fundamental contradiction in our view. Instead of reducing cost and complexity, this mechanism risks becoming a forum for disputes

over eligibility, scope, and valuation.That means more time, more cost, and more pressure on rate payers, not less. It also diverts funding away from core responsibilities like infrastructure and natural hazard resilience, which are already under significant pre- pressure, particularly here. We recommend that the regulatory relief framework be removed entirely from both f- bills.

However, if it is to be retained, we do believe it must be tightened, and tightly limited to genuinely new or bespoke provisions, and must exclude mandated or carried over RMA rules. We support the nationally standardized provisions for efficiency.

However, rigid, rigid standardization rules or, or risks weakening local protections that are well, working well already. For example, in Hastings, our plains production zone has successfully protected our highly productive land and managed lifestyle sub, subdivision.

That has been critical to preventing the gradual loss of our best soils through fragmented development. A standardized rural production zone may not achieve the same outcome. That is a significant risk for our community. If that protection is weakened, the change is effectively irreversible.

We cannot grow on land we have paved over. Once highly productive land is subdivided, we cannot restore it. We support a pathway for bespoke provisions at a local level. We justify... We, we are justified, I need to emphasize that. With decisions undertaken at a local governance level rather than by the minister to avoid unnecessary delay and over-centralization. We are also concerned that the bill does not require mandatory accre- accreditation for hearings commissioners, and I speak for, from that view also as previously accredita- accredited commissioner.

All RMA commissioners currently must hold the Making Good Decisions Accreditation, which ensures baseline competency in law, natural justice, tikanga, and hearing procedures. Without that safeguard, there is a real risk of inconsistent decision-making, which can undermine confidence and increase appeals. We recommend requiring mandatory accreditation or equivalent competency standards for all commissioners.

We recommend a retail, we recommend retail distribution effects are included in the scope of the planning bill. This is particularly important to our community. This is fundamentally how we shape our towns and cities. Managing retail distribution supports compact settlement, housing dens- density around centers, and strong commercial hubs. Without it, development can become scattered, weakening our town centers, increasing infrastructure costs, and putting more pres- pressure on our valuable productive land. For Hastings, this directly rela-

links to protecting our fertile growing soils and maintaining our strong local economy. So in closing, Hastings District Council does support the intent of the reform, but urges the panel to strengthen the bills by explicitly embedding climate change adaptation, providing clear integration between the two bills, allowing realistic timeframes for regional spatial plans, ensuring meaningful Māori participation, avoiding mechanisms that increase legislation and uncertainty.

These changes are not about slowing reform. They're about making sure that the system works in practice, not just in principle. Um, thank you for the opportunity to present, and we're very happy to take any questions. Look, thank you very much, Wendy, and thank you for that submission. Um, obviously a lot of experience, in terms of, climate adaptation and, and mitigation, having been through Cyclone Gabrielle. So look, I might just take the, the privilege of asking the first question, given Hastings is very familiar to

me. Um, when, when you talk about climate adaptation and mitigation being in the goals, w- 'cause it has been raised a few times throughout the submissions, like, how do you genuinely think that would help, and if there was to be, you know, obviously a cyclone again, like, how that actually makes a difference practically?

Well, I think from my perspective, before I hand over to Caleb, who will ask to bring in the technical side of things, from my perspective, it makes a clear message that we are able to take into consideration, those impacts when we're planning for growth, when we're planning where we live and where we work as a community.

Um, the absence of it creates a real risk in that area from my view. Um, so I'm gonna hand over now to Caleb, if you'd like to add anything. Uh, thank you, Mayor Wendy. Uh, s- so I guess o- one of the issues we have in a, I guess the, the, the lower level of governance, in New Zealand is a real lack of national direction about how we manage na- na- natural hazards, how we adapt into the future.

Um, I mean, I submitted to you last year on the, the MPS on natural hazards. It's like, I think, you know, we're thirty-two years into the RMA, we've only just got national direction around natural hazards and how we deal with those. Like, this has to be top-down led.

Those really hard decisions need to come from the top, from the elected members, around how does New Zealand deal with these issues? It needs to be in the statutes. It needs to flow down through the regional spatial plans, and then when we are on the ground preparing regional planning documents, we're backed up when we have to say, "Stop building in dumb places."Because as a, as a consents manager, it is very hard to refuse development in hazard risk locations on a case-by-case basis.

Mm. Because unfortunately under the RMA, which is, you know, you've got three options: avoid, remedy, and mitigate. For the last thirty-two years under the RMA, developers have chosen to mitigate. So they've put in things to, in theory, mitigate that hazard risk.

But what we know now, and especially post-cyclone, is that things such as stopbanks, they can actually fail. Um, and then they can actually make the hazards worse, like we saw in Pakaiwhai where you had breaches up river of the stopbanks. But then because the, the stopbanks down, you know, in, in the, down by the coast in Pakaiwhai, they actually created a, a basin essentially. And, and we had two-story buildings go under five meters of water in a couple of hours. So I guess what we're seeking is some really clear direction from central government, and then needs

to be bought in across both major parties, because this is, this is a key issue affecting New Zealand. It's not just Hawke's Bay. Um, and we can't keep kicking the can down the road. You know, we're facing these issues now. Um, we need that national direction. Tha- thank you, Caleb, for that, and I can, you know, feel the passion there as well. So look, we'll move now to Cameron Luxton, who has a question.

Thank you, Chair. Uh, yeah, just quickly, Hastings, appreciate in your submission you've raised the integration between the Regional Land Transport Plan and the, Spatial Plan. Mm. Could you just... Maniapoto, and the Crown in co-governance and co-management arrangements. At the center of that is Te Ture Whaimana, a statutory instrument that sets the direction for the health and wellbeing of the river. So when we talk about reform, we're not starting from nothing. We are starting from a system that already recognizes our authority, and these bills move in the opposite direction, undermining that authority. That matters because our authority is not symbolic.

It has legal, practical and intergenerational effect. The waters of Taupō and the Upper Waikato are not isolated. They flow from our maunga into our lake, into the Waikato River and through multiple iwi and communities. What happens here does not stay here. Decisions made here flow downstream, and so do the consequences.

These waters are central to our identity, but they are also critical to the environmental, social and economic wellbeing of communities far beyond our rohe. They support ecosystems, they sustain tourism and energy generation, and they are a source of drinking water.

As kaitiaki at the headwaters of the system, we carry a responsibility not just to our own people, but to those downstream as well. With that in mind, the Trust Board strongly opposes these bills, and my kōrero will focus on four key points.

First, these bills weaken environmental protection. Second, they recognize some property rights, but not Māori property rights. Third, they undermine Treaty settlements despite the government's stated commitment to uphold them. And fourth, they remove already limited mechanisms for iwi participation in decision-making. These bills are not the reform that has been promised. They are a step backwards and not in a minor way. This is a fundamental shift in direction. The purpose provisions in

these bills are weaker than what currently exists in the RMA. They shift the focus from protecting the environment toward enabling its use. The new framework replaces clear priorities with a system of trade-offs, where environmental and cultural values can be traded away.

Within that, Māori interests are reduced to one factor among many, even where iwi hold legal rights and responsibilities. That is not neutrality. It is the removal of existing obligations that already exist in law. Key Māori provisions that currently exist under the RMA are either removed or diluted, and the repeated use of softer language like "seek to achieve" weakens accountability. Put simply, these bills lower the standard of environmental

protection in Aotearoa.The bills place strong emphasis on protecting property rights, but that protection is applied selectively. In our case, the Crown is fully aware of our ownership and legal interest in Taupō waters. Those rights are not new. They are well established, and they are significant. And yet they are not properly recognized or provided for in these bills. Just as concerning, the Crown did not engage with the Trust Board in the development of these

provisions. So we are left in a position where property rights are being strengthened in principle but ignored in practice where Māori ownership is concerned. That is a minor gap-- that is not a minor gap. This is a fundamental inconsistency and one the Crown needs to explain.

You cannot claim to strengthen property rights while ignoring those that are already recognized. The bill states that Treaty settlements will be upheld, but in these bills that is largely reduced to requiring settlements to be recognized or taken into account.

That is not how our settlements are designed to operate. Our settlements, including those in the Upper Waikato, are made up of more than a single piece of legislation. They include deeds, accords, protocols, and ongoing relationships with the Crown. Together, those arrangements are intended to work as a whole, with real effect in how decisions are made.

The concept of equivalency is critical here. It requires careful consideration of how those arrangements are carried forward into the new system, so that their integrity, intent, and effect are maintained. These bills do not do that. They change how settlements operate without our agreement.

Instead, they risk reducing settlements to matters that can be acknowledged but not fully given effect to. That creates a real risk that settlements are weakened. At a minimum, the legislation must allow for settlement-specific changes to ensure equivalency is achieved, and it should not proceed until that work has been done in agreement with settlement entities. Without that, this reform cuts across arrangements that have already been agreed.

Joint management agreements, transfers of power and mana whakahono ā-rohe are practical examples of how our resource management relationships operate in the real world. They are mechanisms that enable iwi, local authority-- and local authorities to make decisions together. They give effect to shared responsibility and shared decision-making.

We have also seen in practice that where provisions are not mandatory, progress can stall. That happens when parties are not aligned, and there is no requirement to act. These bills move in the wrong direction. They remove and weaken these types of arrangements and rely more heavily on standard processes that do not reflect existing arrangements. That reduces the requirement for parties to act, and in our experience, when that requirement is not there, decisions do not get made, and that takes us

backwards. We carry responsibilities as kaitiaki, and we do not do that lightly. These bills place those responsibilities at risk. They move away from the direction already agreed through our settlements, and they weaken the systems that give effect to them. That is not acceptable. We cannot support these bills in their current form. They must be amended to properly reflect our rights, our interests and authority already recognized in law.
